# Flour Types Guide

## All-Purpose Flour (10-12% protein)

The workhorse flour for cookies, cakes, pancakes, roux, and general cooking. Protein content varies by brand:

- **King Arthur All-Purpose**: 11.7% protein — on the higher end, produces chewier cookies and sturdier cakes. Our default AP flour. Available at PCC and Costco (25-lb bags at Costco for $12.99).
- **Gold Medal All-Purpose**: 10.5% protein — softer results, better for tender cakes and biscuits. Bleached version available but we avoid bleached flour (chemical treatment with benzoyl peroxide).
- **Bob's Red Mill Unbleached AP**: 10-12% protein — stone-ground, slightly coarser texture. Good for rustic baking. Organic version available.

## Bread Flour (12-14% protein)

Higher protein develops more gluten, creating the chewy, open crumb structure desired in breads.

- **King Arthur Bread Flour**: 12.7% protein — our standard bread flour for sourdough and pizza dough. Consistent quality, milled from hard red winter wheat. We buy the 5-lb bags from PCC ($6.49) or 25-lb bags from King Arthur's website during sales.
- **Central Milling Organic Type 85**: 11.5% protein, high extraction flour that retains some bran and germ. Produces a more flavorful, slightly darker sourdough with better fermentation activity. Alex orders this from Central Milling's website ($8 for 5 lbs).

## 00 Flour (Doppio Zero)

Italian classification based on fineness of grind rather than protein content. Produces silky smooth pasta and thin, crispy-yet-pliable Neapolitan-style pizza.

- **Caputo Tipo 00 "Classica"**: 11.5% protein. Gold standard for pasta. We use this for fresh fettuccine and ravioli. Available at PCC and Metropolitan Market ($5.99 per 2.2 lb bag).
- **Caputo Tipo 00 "Pizzeria"**: 12.5% protein. Designed for pizza dough with 24-72 hour cold fermentation. Higher protein handles the extended fermentation without becoming slack.
- **Key difference from AP**: 00 flour is ground much finer, producing smoother dough that rolls out thinner. Gluten develops differently — stretchy and extensible rather than elastic and snappy.

## Whole Wheat Flour (13-14% protein)

Contains the entire wheat kernel: endosperm, bran, and germ. Nutritionally superior but produces denser baked goods.

- **King Arthur White Whole Wheat**: 13% protein. Milled from hard white wheat (milder flavor than red wheat). We substitute 25-30% of bread flour with this in sourdough for added nutrition without heavy density.
- **Bob's Red Mill Whole Wheat Pastry Flour**: 9.5% protein. Milled from soft white wheat. Excellent for whole wheat pancakes, muffins, and quick breads where tenderness matters.
- **Storage**: Whole wheat flour contains oils from the germ and goes rancid faster than white flour. Store in an airtight container in the freezer (lasts 6-12 months vs. 2-3 months at room temperature).

## Specialty Flours

- **Semolina (durum wheat)**: Very hard wheat, yellow color, coarse grind. Used for extruded pasta shapes (penne, rigatoni) and dusted on pizza peels to prevent sticking. Bob's Red Mill semolina, $4.29/lb at PCC.
- **Rye flour**: Lower gluten than wheat. We keep dark rye flour for feeding the sourdough starter (10% rye boosts fermentation) and for occasional rye bread. Bob's Red Mill Dark Rye, $4.99/lb.
- **Rice flour**: Gluten-free. Used for dusting bannetons (prevents sourdough from sticking better than wheat flour because it doesn't absorb as much moisture). Koda Farms Blue Star Mochiko (sweet rice flour) for mochi.
